avoid: Replacing the W @ X cuBLAS call with a custom full GEMM; high risk of slowdown.
Hardcoding hidden dimensions or tensor pointers; must remain shape-generic for d in [3584, 4608].
Overcomplicating the dispatch; a single fused kernel for the low-rank update is sufficient.
evidence: Candidate is the baseline ATen reference implementation, producing identical latency to torch baseline (speedup ~1.0).
Profile summary confirms bottleneck is multiple ATen GEMM and add launches, with no fusion.
Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0) but no performance gain.
recommended_actions: Keep cuBLAS for W @ X to avoid regressing the dominant full GEMM.
Fuse the rank-16 update (A @ (B.T @ X)) into a single kernel to reduce launch overhead and improve memory locality.
Implement a custom kernel for the low-rank path that computes B.T @ X and then A @ result in one pass, writing directly to the output.
Consider vectorized loads/stores and unrolling for the rank-16 dimensions to maximize bandwidth utilization.

suggested_actions: fuse rank-16 update into the output tensor
test scalar and vectorized rank-16 kernels

avoid: Replacing cuBLAS for the large W @ X GEMM (high risk, unlikely to beat cuBLAS).
Hardcoding hidden dimension d or relying on shape-specific tuning without dispatch.
Ignoring memory bandwidth and focusing only on launch overhead reduction.
evidence: Aggregate speedup 0.987 < 1.0, with per-shape speedups 0.985 (3584), 0.988 (4096), 0.987 (4608), all below baseline.
Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0), so performance regression is not due to numerical issues.
Baseline (torch) likely uses cuBLAS for the rank-16 matmuls, which is highly optimized even for small K=16.
Custom two-kernel approach writes intermediate C (16×d) to global memory and reads it back, adding bandwidth pressure.
Block size 256 may be suboptimal for K=16, leading to low occupancy or wasted threads.
Vector width 4 suggests float4 loads, but stores may not be vectorized, causing inefficient memory transactions.
recommended_actions: Profile the two custom kernels separately to pinpoint whether B.T @ X or Y += A @ C is the bottleneck.
Experiment with smaller block sizes (e.g., 128, 64) to improve occupancy for the thin K=16 dimension.
Ensure both loads and stores use float4 vectorization when d is divisible by 4.
Fuse the two rank-16 kernels into a single kernel that computes Y += A @ (B.T @ X) without materializing C, reducing global memory traffic.
Consider using shared memory to cache A and B tiles to reduce redundant global loads.

candidate.name: fused_rank16_two_kernels
candidate.strategy: Fuse the rank-16 update into two custom kernels: one for B.T @ X (16xd) and one for Y += A @ C, using vectorized loads and tiled outer product for K=16. Keep cuBLAS for W @ X. Expect reduction in launch overhead and better memory locality, aiming for speedup >1.0.

suggested_fixes: Add a device consistency check: TORCH_CHECK(W.device() == X.device() && X.device() == A.device() && A.device() == B.device(), "all tensors must be on the same device");
Call t = t.contiguous(); after matmul to ensure row-major layout before passing to the custom kernel.
Either implement the custom kernel for B.T @ X or update the strategy description to reflect the use of cuBLAS for that step.
warnings: No check that all input tensors are on the same CUDA device; may cause runtime errors if tensors are on different devices.
Intermediate tensor `t` from matmul is not explicitly made contiguous; custom kernel assumes row-major layout, which could be violated if matmul returns a non-contiguous tensor.
Strategy description mentions a custom kernel for B.T @ X, but the code uses cuBLAS matmul instead, potentially missing the intended optimization.

name: fused_rank16_two_kernels

suggested_actions: try float4 stores when d is divisible by 4
fall back to ATen if numerical error appears

strategy: Fuse the rank-16 update into two custom kernels: one for B.T @ X (16xd) and one for Y += A @ C, using vectorized loads and tiled outer product for K=16. Keep cuBLAS for W @ X. Expect reduction in launch overhead and better memory locality, aiming for speedup >1.0.
